coredata.dc:title: Water hyacinth powder-assisted in-situ fabrication of visible light responsive CQDs/BiOCl heterojunctions with exceptional photocatalytic detoxification performance

coredata.dc:description: As a typical Bi-based photocatalyst, BiOCl has been received increasing attention because of its many advantages. However, poor visible light response ability and high recombination rate of photoexcited charge pairs significantly inhibit the intense application of BiOCl, therefore, it is considerable to develop a strategy to overcome the inherent shortcomings of BiOCl to boost its photocatalytic properties. The former works have strongly affirmed that coupling carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with BiOCl to construct heterojunctions is a practical method to boost the photoactivity of BiOCl. In this demonstration, water hyacinth powder was used to provide raw material for CQDs, and CQDs/OVs-BiOCl heterostructures were in-situ fabricated through a solvothermal synthesis. The successful modification of CQDs on BiOCl and the formation of abundant oxygen vacancies (OVs) were confirmed by several analytical methods. The test results of photoelectric property reveal that CQDs/OVs-BiOCl has faster photogenerated charge separation rate than the reference BiOCl. Photoactivity of all the samples was examined by destruction of rhodamine B (RhB) and defluorination of perfluorooctanoic acid (PFOA). The CQDs/OVs-BiOCl heterostructures exhibit the ascendant photocatalytic destruction activity. Among them, the degradation efficiency of RhB over 1%CQDs/OVs-BiOCl under visible light irradiation is 2.6 folds of that of over the reference BiOCl. Besides, the defluorination rate of PFOA on 1%CQDs/OVs-BiOCl under UV light irradiation is 1.4 times of that of on BiOCl. The cyclic experimental results solidly prove that the CQDs/OVs-BiOCl samples have outstanding stability and good application prospect. Free radical detection experiments reveal that 1%CQDs/OVs-BiOCl reaction system produces more active species than the reference BiOCl system, and ·O2 - is the most important active substance. Additionally, the total organic carbon (TOC) test strongly demonstrate that RhB can be effectively mineralized into inorganic small substances on 1%CQDs/OVs-BiOCl under light shine. Based on the results of all the characterizations, a photocatalytic improvement mechanism for CQDs/OVs-BiOCl was reasonably elaborated. This work provides a new approach to obtain CQDs to modify BiOCl, which not only improves the photocatalytic degradation activity of BiOCl, but also achieves the purpose of use waste to treat waste.

head.ce:title: Water hyacinth powder-assisted in-situ fabrication of visible light responsive CQDs/BiOCl heterojunctions with exceptional photocatalytic detoxification performance

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As a typical Bi-based photocatalyst, BiOCl has been received increasing attention because of its many advantages. However, poor visible light response ability and high recombination rate of photoexcited charge pairs significantly inhibit the intense application of BiOCl, therefore, it is considerable to develop a strategy to overcome the inherent shortcomings of BiOCl to boost its photocatalytic properties. The former works have strongly affirmed that coupling carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with BiOCl to construct heterojunctions is a practical method to boost the photoactivity of BiOCl. In this demonstration, water hyacinth powder was used to provide raw material for CQDs, and CQDs/OVs-BiOCl heterostructures were in-situ fabricated through a solvothermal synthesis. The successful modification of CQDs on BiOCl and the formation of abundant oxygen vacancies (OVs) were confirmed by several analytical methods. The test results of photoelectric property reveal that CQDs/OVs-BiOCl has faster photogenerated charge separation rate than the reference BiOCl. Photoactivity of all the samples was examined by destruction of rhodamine B (RhB) and defluorination of perfluorooctanoic acid (PFOA). The CQDs/OVs-BiOCl heterostructures exhibit the ascendant photocatalytic destruction activity. Among them, the degradation efficiency of RhB over 1%CQDs/OVs-BiOCl under visible light irradiation is 2.6 folds of that of over the reference BiOCl. Besides, the defluorination rate of PFOA on 1%CQDs/OVs-BiOCl under UV light irradiation is 1.4 times of that of on BiOCl. The cyclic experimental results solidly prove that the CQDs/OVs-BiOCl samples have outstanding stability and good application prospect. Free radical detection experiments reveal that 1%CQDs/OVs-BiOCl reaction system produces more active species than the reference BiOCl system, and ·O2 - is the most important active substance. Additionally, the total organic carbon (TOC) test strongly demonstrate that RhB can be effectively mineralized into inorganic small substances on 1%CQDs/OVs-BiOCl under light shine. Based on the results of all the characterizations, a photocatalytic improvement mechanism for CQDs/OVs-BiOCl was reasonably elaborated. This work provides a new approach to obtain CQDs to modify BiOCl, which not only improves the photocatalytic degradation activity of BiOCl, but also achieves the purpose of use waste to treat waste.

ce:section.ce:para: Nowadays, with the continuous improvement of industrial production level, the treatment of various pollutants discharged at the end of the industrial chain is ignored, resulting in a heavy burden on the ecological environment, which will eventually become a more restrictive living space for human beings [1,2]. Therefore, human development should erase the stain left on the ecological environment in time while advancing. Taking a cue from plant photosynthesis, the researchers have demonstrated that harnessing the limitless sunlight to excite semiconductors to produce non-toxic and effective chemical substances is one of the ideal approaches to decontaminate the environment. Crucially, photocatalysts and luminous intensity are two breakthrough points to advance the efficiency of semiconductor photocatalysts [3–6]. In brief, semiconductor photocatalysts excited under light can produce electrons (e-) and holes (h+) with strong redox ability, which can be utilized to accelerate the mineralization of organic pollutants and destroy harmful substances [7,8]. Accordingly, development of new and highly efficient photocatalysts is a hotpot [9–12]. Meanwhile, broadening the light absorption range of photocatalyst and improving the separation rate of photoexcited charge carriers are necessary breakthroughs to advance the efficient application of photocatalytic technology under macroscopic conditions.

Bismuth-containing materials including BiOX (X = Cl, Br, I), BiPO4 and Bi2MoO6 are favored by researchers owing to their outstanding photocatalytic properties through visible light or UV irradiation [13–16]. Among them, BiOCl has a unique nanosheet structure, which is assembled by [Bi2O2]2+ and chloride ions. The strong internal electrostatic field between layers can facilitate the fast transfer of photoinduced electrons [17,18]. Nevertheless, the inherent forbidden band of BiOCl is relative wide (about 3.2 eV), which requires higher energy to stimulate the electron transition in the bulk, and the low conduction edge potential leads to the excited electrons cannot directly participate in the reduction reaction and easily recombine with holes. These obvious inherent deficiencies gravely inhibit the practical application of BiOCl [19–21], therefore, it is imperative to compensate these shortcomings to drastically boost the photoactivity of BiOCl. At present, many methods have been attested to be effective in drastically altering the photocatalytic efficiency of semiconductors, such as heterojunctions construction [22], morphology optimization [23], atomic doping [24] and construction of defects [25]. Therein, construction of heterojunctions is the most generally adopted strategy because of its simple preparation method, cost-effective, and can conspicuously enhance the performance of photocatalysts [26–32]. For example, Gao’s group prepared a flower-like n-Bi2O3/n-BiOCl photocatalyst by a one-pot method. The sample with optimal ratio of Bi2O3/BiOCl can significantly promote the degradation of various simulated pollutant. The boosted photocatalytic performance is primarily owing to the formation of heterojunctions, resulting in building up interfacial electric field, widening optical response and expediting the separation and migration of photoinduced charge pairs [33].

Defect engineering is also a means which can enhance the photoactivity of semiconductors, and vacancy is one kind of point defect, which is the most ordinary and easy to form in defect engineering [34]. Generally, vacancies include cationic vacancies (Ti, Si, C) and anionic vacancies (O, N, S) [35]. In particular, introduction of OVs is the most common strategy in oxygen-containing materials. The OVs can establish a new energy level beneath the conduction band (CB) and optimize the band structure, thus efficiency promoting photoexcited charge pairs to separate and migrate [36,37]. Additionally, OVs has a strong ability to capture electrons, and can be used as reaction sites to activate more O2 to convert into superoxide radical (·O2 -) [38]. Compared with most metal oxides, the bond energy of Bi–O in BiOCl is low, and oxygen atoms are easy to escape from the (010) plane after bond breaking, thus forming OVs, which facilitates the capture of photoexcited electrons to improve the photocatalytic performance [39]. Therefore, it is a significant idea to boost the catalytic performance of BiOCl by introducing suitable OVs while constructing heterojunctions.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are a class of zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials with remarkable fluorescence properties, which are composed of dispersed spheroidal carbon particles. They have attracted wide attentions owing to their characteristics of low toxicity, environmentally friendly, good water solubility and biocompatibility [40,41], which endow with CQDs high universality and potential application prospects. Meanwhile, CQDs come from a wide range of sources and can be obtained from waste or hazardous substance, thus greatly enriching the conversion mode of materials in the environment [42]. Owing to rapidly photogenerated electrons transport, excellent electron storage properties and good up-conversion photoluminescence, CQDs have become an attractive option for modifying the structure of other semiconductor materials and improving photocatalytic performance [43]. To elucidate, Qin and coworkers synthesized CQDs/BiOCl nanocomposites with abundant OVs by a one-pot method utilizing maltitol as carbon source. The best sample can effectively degrade 72% tetracycline hydrochloride after 1 h through the synergistic effect of point discharge effect and OVs produced via CQDs, benefiting from the highly efficiency separation and transfer of photoinduced charge pairs [44].

Water hyacinth (WH), once known as an "environmental warrior", can not only remove cyanide and organics from industrial wastewater, but also decontaminate a variety of heavy metals (Cd, Cr, Pb) in waterbody. However, the reproduction capacity of WH is so horrible that it can cover the whole water in a short time, resulting in the serious destruction of the food chain in the water. Importantly, WH cannot completely degrade all the pollutants, and it is easy to form a "heavy metal layer" after decay, which will not only accelerate the deterioration of waterbody quality, but also cause the extinction of aquatic life [45]. Therefore, it is necessary to reasonably control the growth of WH or highly efficient utilization of WH resources.

Inspired by the above thought, herein, we synthesized BiOCl nanocomposites modified with CQDs by a conventional solvothermal method using WH as the source for CQDs. The prepared CQDs/OVs-BiOCl photocatalysts were verified by the various characterization techniques. Interestingly, compared to the reference BiOCl, the CQDs/OVs-BiOCl nanocomposite has a larger specific surface area, more abundant OVs and faster photogenerated charge pairs separation rate. The presence of CQDs accelerates the transfer of the photoinduced electrons. During the photocatalytic degradation of RhB and PFOA, the synergistic effect of CQDs and OVs greatly promotes the formation of ·O2 -, thus CQDs/OVs-BiOCl nanocomposites display ascendant photocatalytic degradation activity. In view of the key role of CQDs and OVs in holding excellent photoactivity, the enhanced photocatalytic activity of the prepared CQDs/OVs-BiOCl nanocomposites was rationally discussed.

Water hyacinth powder (WHP) was used as carbon source to synthesize CQDs/OVs-BiOCl samples with abundant OVs by a solvothermal method. Specifically, the flow chart of CQDs/OVs-BiOCl photocatalysts assisted by WHP was displayed in Scheme 1 . First, the fresh WH was spread out in the air circulation and dried in the shade, and then was ground into fine powder with a grinder. The useable WHP can be obtained through the sieve of a 100-mesh sieve.

5.0000 g Bi(NO3)3·5H2O was added into a beaker containing 40 mL glacial acetic acid and treated by ultrasound until Bi(NO3)3·5H2O was completely dissolved, and then desired WHP was dispersed into the above transparent solution to form suspension A. 0.7679 g KCl was dissolved in 20 mL deionized water and tardily dripped into solution A through a constant-flow pump, then the mixed system was tempestuously stirred for 12 h until the dissolution-precipitation equilibrium. The evenly mixed system was decanted to a polytetrafluoroethylene lined hydrothermal reactor and retained at 180 °C for 24 h. After autoclave cooling down, the products were fully washed and filtered using a Brinell funnel. Finally, all the products were dried at 60 °C for 12 h and was triturated evenly. According to different mass ratio of WHP and BiOCl, the prepared photocatalytic materials were labeled as reference BiOCl (without WHP), 0.5%, 1%, 1.5% and 2%, respectively.

Desired WHP was added into a beaker containing 40 mL glacial acetic acid and then was stirred for 1 h. 20 mL deionized water was dropped into the above beaker through a constant-flow pump and continued to stir for 12 h. The obtained suspension was decanted into a polytetrafluoroethylene lined hydrothermal reactor and treated in the same heating procedure as above. After autoclave cooling down to room temperature, the supernatant in the hydrothermal reactor was slowly evaporated to obtain solid. Finally, the solid was ground to obtain the reference CQDs.
All characterization methods and photoactivity measurements were described in the Supporting Information.

Phase structure and crystallinity of all the photocatalysts were determined by a XRD instrument and the outcomes were displayed in Fig. 1 A. Diffraction peaks of all the prepared photocatalysts are accordance with the tetragonal BiOCl (JCPDS: 06–0249). The main diffraction peak can be attributed to the (001), (101), (110) and (102) crystal plane, separately. The diffraction peaks are sharp, and no diffraction peaks for impurities were detected, indicating good crystallinity and high purity of BiOCl [46]. No diffraction peaks for CQDs were scanned in the sample, which can be assigned to the low loading and high dispersion of CQDs. Impressively, samples with high WHP content have stronger peak strength of the (001) crystal plane of BiOCl, which can be allocated to the influence of CQDs produced in the hydrothermal process on the crystal growth of BiOCl, but CQDs has no obvious effect on the crystal type of BiOCl [41]. Furthermore, the shift of diffraction peak of the (001) crystal planes was observed through the enlarged image (Fig. 1B). Compared with the BiOCl, the peaks of CQDs/BiOCl (001) crystal plane shift to a lower angle to some extent. Based on the Bragg formula (2dsinθ = nλ), lower diffraction angle will lead to larger crystal plane spacing, implies that the attachment of CQDs onto BiOCl and powerful chemical interaction with BiOCl, rather than a simple physical mixture of CQDs and BiOCl [47]. The presence of CQDs loading on BiOCl surface and forming CQDs/BiOCl heterojunctions will be further elucidated by HRTEM, PL spectrum, FT-IR and XPS.

Structural characteristics of CQDs produced by water hyacinth powder were further investigated by Raman spectroscopy and particle size analysis. Fig. S2A shows that two characteristic peaks at 1340 cm-1 (D band) and 1550 cm-1 (G band) in Raman spectra. In general, the D band and G band represent the defect of the carbon material and the in-plane vibration of CC, separately [51,52]. The sharpness of the peaks further indicates the high crystallinity of the prepared CQDs. Moreover, the particle size of the prepared CQDs was tested, and the results were shown in Fig. S2B. The average D, area-average D and volume-average D of CQDs is 6.6, 6.5 and 6.6 μm, respectively [53,54]. Ulteriorly, according to a variety of optical properties of CQDs, the real existence of CQDs was further verified, such as photoluminescence and optical absorption, thus PL test was employed, and the results were shown in Fig. 3 A. The suspension system after hydrothermal reaction were centrifuged to obtain the supernatant, which can be used for PL detection. The test results show that a strong peak appears at 459 nm for CQDs solution, while no peak was detected in the supernatant of the 1% sample and BiOCl, which could be attributed to the fact that the generated few CQDs had been successfully modified on the surface of BiOCl and no CQDs were dispersed into the solution. Besides, CQDs has photoluminescence characteristics. Two liquids were put on the thin layer chromatography silica gel plate and were irradiated by an ultraviolet lamp. The light is blue light, which is consistent with the blue light in the figure, firmly confirming the existence of CQDs in the 1% sample. The samples were further tested via FT-IR (Fig. 3B). The surface of CQDs contains many functional groups, such as –OH, –COOH, which have significant effects on the optical properties of CQDs. The stretching vibration peak at 3438 cm-1 and 1622 cm-1 derives from surface hydroxyl group and H2O adsorbed on the catalytic material, respectively [55]. In addition, the stretching vibration peak of unsaturated C–H at 2930 cm-1 is caused by the incomplete carbonization of WHP in the preparation of CQDs [25]. Compared to the reference CQDs, no C–H bond vibration was found in the 1% samples, which can be assigned to low loading of CQDs. The peak at 1719 cm-1 represents the CO bond breathing modes of CQDs. The bond length of CO (0.12 nm) is shorter than that of Bi–O bond (0.19 nm), and the bond energy of CO (755.7 kJ/mol) is higher than that of Bi–O bond (337.3 kJ/mol). Accordingly, CQDs can easily snatch O atoms by forming CO bonds at the CQDs/BiOCl interface, resulting in the fracture of Bi–O bonds in the lattice, which is conducive to the conversion of surface hydroxy-oxygen and OVs, regulating the concentration of surface OVs, thus promoting the photocatalytic activity [56]. The signal at 1390 cm-1 is triggered by the D-band of CQDs. The sharp peak at 524 cm-1 in the 1% sample symbolizes the stretching modes of Bi–O bond [57]. Therefore, combined with the characterization results of PL and FT-IR, substantially demonstrating that CQDs/OVs-BiOCl photocatalysts have been successfully in-situ prepared.

XPS was used to explore the elements state of the CQDs/OVs-BiOCl photocatalytic material. Fig. 4 A exhibits the full spectra of all the catalysts, and the presence of C, Bi, O and Cl elements was detected. Therein, C 1s spectra well reveals the presence of CC and C–C carbon (284.8 eV), C–O carbon (286.2 eV), and CO groups (288.5 eV) in the 1% sample (Fig. S3) [58]. Commonly, Fig. 4B illustrates that the Bi 4f spectrum contains two core peaks which can be assigned to Bi 4f7/2 and Bi 4f5/2 at 159.2 eV and 164.6 eV, separately [59]. Two Bi 4f peaks indicate that Bi exists in the form of Bi3+ in the sample. Fig. 4C shows O 1s orbital of the reference BiOCl can be fitted into three main peaks, which are located at 530.7 eV, 532.3 eV and 533.4 eV, belong to Bi–O lattice oxygen, OVs and surface hydroxyl, separately [60]. The positions of the three fitting peaks in the 1% sample O1s appears slight shift compared with those of the reference BiOCl (Fig. 4D), specifically lattice oxygen (530.4 eV), OVs (532.2 eV) and surface hydroxyl (533.6 eV). Particularly, the content of Bi–O lattice oxygen in the 1% sample considerably decreases compared with the BiOCl (52.34%→41.62%), while the content of OVs and surface hydroxyl group increases owing to the fracture of Bi–O bond in the 1% sample and formation of OVs, resulting in the increase of Bi electron cloud density and butterfly effect on surrounding atoms. Commonly, high OVs and surface hydroxyl groups content will promote photocatalytic performance by facilitating the separation of photoexcited carriers and providing more reaction space [61]. Moreover, the in-situ generation of CQDs on the surface of BiOCl induces BiOCl to produce surface oxygen in the form of OVs, hydroxyl radicals and water. Fig. 4E exhibits the state of Cl 2P, and the binding energy is 198.3 eV, indicating that the states of Cl elements are all ionic states. Accordingly, the interaction between CQDs and BiOCl will give rise to the change of OVs on the surface of BiOCl and enhance the separation of BiOCl photoinduced carriers, thus improving the degradation efficiency towards pollutant [62]. Furthermore, the generation of OVs on the samples was visually and strongly confirmed using EPR experiment and the observation results were displayed in Fig. 4F. All the tested samples generate EPR signals at g = 2.004, which strongly confirms the authenticity of OVs existence on the material surface. The OVs in the reference BiOCl can be attributed to the escape of oxygen atoms during hydrothermal process, while the OVs in CQDs/BiOCl also include the absence of oxygen associated with the generation of CQDs during the formation of BiOCl. The EPR signal increases with the increasing of CQDs content, but excessive CQDs content will result in reduced EPR signal [63,64]. Specifically, EPR signal peak of the 1% sample is the strongest, suggesting that the sample has the richest surface OVs. Particularly, CQDs with conversion fluorescence effect can convert the absorbed visible light into ultraviolet light, which is beneficial to activate BiOCl to produce photogenerated carriers. Electrons will be captured by OVs and react with adsorbed O2 to produce ·O2 -. Therefore, abundant OVs will remarkably boost the photocatalytic efficiency of BiOCl.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties and band structure assessment
ce:section.ce:para: Light response properties of the prepared catalysts were investigated through UV–Vis DRS. As depicted in Fig. 5 A, all the catalysts appear strong absorption in the ultraviolet region and the optical absorption edge is about 370 nm. After introduction of CQDs, the optical response performance of CQDs/OVs-BiOCl is slightly improved between 370 nm and 600 nm (inset of Fig. 5A), which is conducive to the absorption of more light during the photodegradation reaction to stimulate the separation and migration of photoinduced e-/h+ pairs. Meanwhile, band gap (Eg) of the semiconductor was calculated to be 3.24 e, which is in good accordance with the Tauc plot method (Fig. 5B). The successful modification of CQDs onto BiOCl will not cause the change of BiOCl structure, which can be attributed to the low content of CQDs and concentrate on the surface of BiOCl crystals or shallow lattice [65,66]. Besides, band structure is an essential information in the study of semiconductor material properties. The flat-band potential is one of the most vital starting points for determining the band location. The Mott-Schottky test was carried out with calomel as the reference electrode to identify the relevant information of BiOCl and the corresponding results were exhibited in Fig. 5C. The tangent line of curve has a positive slope, demonstrating that BiOCl is an n-type semiconductor, namely, manifesting electricity from the movement of free electrons in the semiconductors [67]. The intersection of the curve with the X-axis is the position of flat potential and the value is about -1.03 V (Hg/Hg2Cl2, pH = 7). Commonly, the conduction band (CB) potential is 0.1 V more negative than flat potential, and CB potential of the reference BiOCl is about -0.48 V (vs. NHE, pH = 7) by conversion to normal hydrogen electrode (NHE). Simultaneously, the valence band (VB) edge potential of BiOCl is obtained to be 2.76 V following the formula ECB = EVB - Eg. Fig. 5D depicted that the intersection point between the tangent line of the curve’s maximum absorption edge and the baseline extension line is the VB edge potential of the semiconductor according to the test principle [68,69]. Accordingly, the VB potential of BiOCl is determined to be 2.76 V by VB-XPS after C1s calibration, which accords well with the VB edge potential calculated by Mott-Schottky.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Separation behavior of photoinduced charge pairs
ce:section.ce:para: Photoexcited carriers separation behavior of the prepared photocatalysts under full wavelength light was studied by SPS technique. As presented in Fig. 6 A, all the materials have strong SPS absorption peaks in the UV light region with wavelength ranging from 310 nm to 370 nm, and the CQDs/OVs-BiOCl photocatalytic material has stronger SPS response than BiOCl. Evidently, SPS response capacity of the sample reaches the highest when the mass ratio of water hyacinth powder/BiOCl is 1%, implying that the sample has the best charge pairs separation behavior [70]. In the visible light region with wavelength ranging from 650 nm to 720 nm, the sample exhibits a weak SPS signal response, which can be assigned to the introduction of CQDs and OVs on the BiOCl surface. In general, the presence of OVs can establish a new energy level, resulting in the emergence of SPS signal in 650 nm to 720 nm. Moreover, the position of new energy level can be calculated by the formula: Eg = 1240/λ, λ is the peak corresponding to the maximum absorption wavelength (685 nm) [71], the band gap between the VB edge potential and the new energy level is 1.81 V, so the potential position of the OVs level is located at 0.95 V. When the edge potential of OVs is much higher than that of the conduction potential (-0.48 V), the photogenerated electrons firstly reach the new OVs level during the transition, and some of the electrons will be directly captured by OVs, which effectively inhibits the rapid recombination of photoinduced charge pairs and improves the separation efficiency of photogenerated carriers. O2 adsorbed on the surface of the catalyst can be rapidly converted into ·O2 - under the action of photoexcited electrons to participate in destruction of the pollutants [72]. Furthermore, the degradation experiments for pollutants were carried out in the liquid phase environment, so the separation rate of photoinduced e-/h+ pairs in the liquid phase environment was investigated by transient photocurrent and electrochemical impedance tests (Fig. 6B). After five alternating illuminations, the instantaneous photocurrent of CQDs/OVs-BiOCl photocatalyst is stronger than that of the BiOCl, and the electrical signal formed is stable. CQDs/OVs-BiOCl photocatalysts has a smaller arc radius in the impedance map compared with BiOCl (Fig. 6C), representing a lower charge-transfer resistance [73,74]. In conclusion, introduction of CQDs has made a significant contribution to promote the charge pairs transfer efficiency of BiOCl. 1%CQDs/OVs-BiOCl shows the best performance and is expected to hold the most prominent activity in the photocatalytic degradation experiment.

Active species produced by the reference BiOCl and CQDs/OVs-BiOCl materials under light irradiation were studied using EPR technique. Fig. 8 A illustrates that the characteristic peaks of DMPO -·O2 - appear in both photocatalytic systems, indicating that ·O2 - produces in the photocatalytic system under light illumination. Based on the reference BiOCl, the 1% sample has a higher DMPO-·O2 - signal. Generally, ·O2 - is produced by the reaction of a single electron with O2. The CQDs/OVs-BiOCl photocatalytic system produces a higher concentration of ·O2 -, representing that decoration of CQDs can induce more photoinduced electrons to react with O2. The interesting phenomenon can be appointed to the delocalized conjugated structure of CQDs, which can more easily transfer photogenerated electrons, thus inhibiting the recombination of photoexcited charge pairs and promoting the destruction of pollutants [75]. Besides, Fig. 8B illustrates that weak DMPO-·OH characteristic peaks were also recorded, implying that ·OH also exists in both photocatalytic systems. The capture experiment was performed to explore the role of active species in the degradation process. As shown in Fig. 8C, the trapping agents were benzoquinone (BQ), ammonium oxalate (AO) and isopropyl alcohol (IPA), which can capture ·O2 -, h+ and ·OH, separately [76]. Compared with the blank experiment without trapping agent, detoxification of RhB on the 1% samples is severely inhibited after addition of BQ, indicating that ·O2 - is the main active species to destroy pollutants, followed by h+ and ·OH. Meanwhile, NBT-·O2 - experiments were performed to determine the relative content of ·O2 - (Fig. 8D). Since the reaction of NBT with ·O2 - will produce monomethyl accumulation (yellow), which will then be reduced to form blue precipitates (dimethyl accumulation), the richer ·O2 - in the reaction will consume more NBT, namely, the corresponding peak intensity of NBT will be lower. Apparently, the content of ·O2 - in the 1% sample system is slightly higher compared with the BiOCl system, and high content of ·O2 - is conducive to accelerate the degradation of pollutants, which fits well with the photocatalytic activity.

TOC is a comprehensive index which can indicate the total amount of organic matter in water body by carbon content. Accordingly, it is used as a considerable reference index to assess the degree of organic pollution in water [77,78]. TOC experiments were employed to explore the actual degradation of RhB (Fig. 9 ). Eventually, the TOC removal efficiency of RhB on the 1% sample reaches 48% after 80 min illumination. The TOC experiment strongly supports the effective degradation of organic pollutants into non-toxic small molecules such as CO2 and H2O through photocatalytic technology with the help of 1%CQDs/OVs-BiOCl, the sample has promising application prospect.

Based on the analysis of all the experimental results, the photocatalytic mechanism of CQDs/OVs-BiOCl was elaborated and illustrated in Fig. 10 . When the photocatalytic material is exposed to light, the photogenerated electrons in BiOCl can be excited and jump from VB to CB. Due to the CB edge potential of BiOCl (-0.48 V) is more negative than that of E(·O2 -/O2) = -0.33 V, those photogenerated electrons can be used to reduce oxygen at the CB position [79,80]. The content of OVs in the reference BiOCl is lower and have weaker ability to capture electrons, thus the reference BiOCl holds low photocatalytic activity. However, after introduction of CQDs, the separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs can be promoted due to the good up-conversion photoluminescence and fast photoexcited electrons transfer ability of CQDs. The characteristics of CQDs can also transform long-wavelength light and emit short-wavelength light, so that the full spectrum of sunlight can be used to achieve efficient energy conversion [81,82]. For CQDs/OVs-BiOCl photocatalyst, a OVs level can be well established below the CB, the photoinduced electrons can be excited by light and jump to the new level created by OVs, OVs can directly capture part of the photogenerated electrons at this level, and other electron can be further excited to the conduction band [39,83]. Subsequently, when the accumulated electrons in the conduction band cannot directly participate in the reaction and can be captured by OVs. The captured electrons will react with O2 adsorbed on the surface of the catalytic material to generate ·O2 -, destructing the target pollutants into small molecular compounds. Simultaneously, the CO bond formed by CQDs at the interface can deprive oxygen atoms from BiOCl, resulting in the fracture of Bi–O bond in the lattice of BiOCl. Due to the special delocalized conjugated structure of CQDs, the photogenerated electrons will easily transfer to CQDs to promote the formation of ·O2 -, thus greatly accelerating the destruction of pollutants and significantly improving the photocatalytic activity [84,85]. Besides, the VB edge potential of CQDs/OVs-BiOCl is more positive than E(·OH/H2O) = 2.37 V and E(·OH/OH-) = 1.99 V, so h+ can directly attack the pollutant or convert OH-/H2O into strongly oxidizing ·OH. The ·OH formed can mineralize the pollutants into small molecules and achieving efficient degradation of pollutants [86].

In summary, CQDs were successfully in-situ constructed on BiOCl through a solvothermal method using WHP as carbon source, and regulable oxygen vacancies were introduced. The synergistic effect of CQDs and oxygen vacancies promotes transfer of electrons, accelerates the separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs, improves the levels of ·O2 - and ·OH, and enhances the destruction ability for pollutants. Among all the samples prepared, 1%CQDs/BiOCl presents the most ascendant photocatalytic activity in degradation of RhB and defluorination of PFOA. TOC experiments strongly verify that RhB can be effectively decomposed into small molecules over 1%CQDs/BiOCl. This work offers a new strategy for the efficient utilization of waste resources to enhance photocatalytic activity of semiconductor photocatalysts.
